mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Defender V8 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Defender V8 engine typically costs £2,500–£5,500 supply only depending on era, with the late 4.0 sitting at £2,500–£5,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,300. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Defender V8 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: That depends on which of the four engine configurations your Defender has. The 3.5 carburettor (1983–1985) is widely considered the most durable configuration — simple, no ECU, easy to fix. The 3.9 EFI (1986–1994) adds some complexity but retains the same core reliability. The late 4.0 (1995–1997) introduces liner-slippage risk — a well-documented issue — but can be permanently cured with top-hat liners. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Defender V8?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cylinder liner slippage on the 4.0 V8 (coolant loss, overheating), timing chain wear across all eras (rattling on startup), and carburettor/EFI system issues on 3.5/3.9 variants (age-related rubber perishing and electrical corrosion). The 4.0's liner risk is the most significant financial exposure.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Defender V8 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £10,000 Defender V8 with 4.0 engine, a £4,000 rebuild is 40% of the car's value — worth it. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same rebuild exceeds our 60% Land Rover threshold — get a scrap valuation first. For 3.5 and 3.9 examples, the simpler engine often makes repair more viable relative to vehicle value.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Defender V8 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Land Rover's VIN plate and engine block stamping. As a rule of thumb: pre-1986 is 3.5 carburettor, 1986–1994 is 3.9 EFI, 1995–1997 is 4.0 GEMS/Thor, and 1998 (50th Anniversary) is late 3.9 or 4.0. The engine code is stamped on the block's left-hand side near the dipstick.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 4.0 V8 liner issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Top-hat liner installation (£1,500–£2,500) is a permanent cure for the classic Rover V8 liner-slip problem. If the block has already cracked or the liners have dropped significantly, replacement may be the only viable option (£2,500–£5,500 for a reconditioned long-block). At the first sign of coolant loss, book an inspection immediately.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Defender V8 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Vehicle is a keeper — value justifies a one-time bottom-end rebuild
Almost always worth fixing — these are simple systems to service
Only if the vehicle has exceptional value (e.g., 50th Anniversary, NAS-spec)
Block replacement — often £3,000–£5,500 for a replacement long-block

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Defender V8? Check These First
buyIf: * 3.5 or 3.9 carb/EFI with documented history — the simplest, most robust configurations
* 4.0 with top-hat liners already fitted — problem solved permanently
* Full service history with regular oil changes (the Rover V8 loves clean oil)
* A pre-1995 3.9 if you want the sweet spot of power and simplicity
avoidIf: * 4.0 with no liner history and evidence of coolant loss (liner risk still present)
* Any Rover V8 with oil cooler blocked or cooling system neglected (overheating kills these engines)
* Poor compression on any cylinder — the Rover V8 is forgiving but not invincible
* 50th Anniversary cars with 4.0 and no rebuild history — the scarcity premium doesn't fix the engineering

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Rover V8 is one of the most straightforward engine families to own — if you buy the right era and maintain it properly. The 3.5 and 3.9 carburettor/EFI variants are rock-solid; the 4.0 introduced liner risk but a top-hat rebuild eliminates it permanently. In our view, a top-hatted 4.0 is the ultimate Defender V8 — but only if that work is already done.

cta.label: → Compare Defender V8 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Defender V8 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup; monitor oil pressure after start
* Service History — Including which engine era (3.5 carb / 3.9 EFI / 4.0 GEMS/Thor)
* Coolant Check — Any sign of coolant loss; pressurisation; oil contamination (liner warning)
* Oil Pressure — Must hold steady at idle and rise cleanly with RPM; any warning light = investigate
* Carb/EFI System — 3.5: check choke operation; 3.9: check cold start and idle stability
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? (Rover V8s can be thirsty)
* Exhaust Smoke — Blue smoke on start or over-run indicates valve seals; white smoke = coolant ingress
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (EFI sensors can be intermittent)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.
